questions.question: Explain the difference between a singly linked list and a doubly linked list. Also state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Singly linked list has one pointer (NEXT) per node; doubly has two (PREV and NEXT)
Advantage singly: uses less memory (one pointer per node)
Advantage doubly: allows bidirectional traversal
Disadvantage singly: cannot traverse backwards
Disadvantage doubly: extra memory for previous pointers and complex operations

questions.question: Describe the steps to insert a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list. Also mention its time complexity.
keyPoints: Create a new node dynamically
Assign data to the new node
Set new node's next pointer to current head
Update head pointer to point to new node
Time complexity is O(1) - constant time operation

questions.question: What are the advantages of linked lists over arrays? Also mention two disadvantages of linked lists.
keyPoints: Advantages: dynamic size (no fixed size), efficient insertion/deletion, no memory wastage
Disadvantage 1: no random access (must traverse)
Disadvantage 2: extra memory overhead for pointers
Additional disadvantage: not cache-friendly

questions.question: Write an algorithm to delete a node from a singly linked list when the node to be deleted is given as a pointer.
keyPoints: If node to delete is head: move head to next and free old head
If node is in middle/end: traverse to find previous node
Set previous node's next to node's next
Free the memory of deleted node
Handle edge case when node doesn't exist

questions.question: What is a circular linked list? Differentiate between singly circular and doubly circular linked lists.
keyPoints: Circular linked list: last node's next points to head instead of NULL
Singly circular: one-way traversal, last node points to first
Doubly circular: bidirectional, last node's next to head, first node's prev to last
Used in round-robin scheduling, continuous loops

questions.question: Explain the concept of a dummy header node in linked lists. How does it simplify operations?
keyPoints: Dummy header is a placeholder node at the beginning without actual data
Eliminates special case for insertion/deletion at first position
Head always points to dummy, making all insertions uniform
Simplifies code as no separate handling needed for empty list operations

questions.question: Describe Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. How does it detect a loop in a linked list?
keyPoints: Also known as Tortoise and Hare algorithm
Use two pointers: slow (moves 1 step) and fast (moves 2 steps)
If there's a loop, fast will eventually meet slow
Time complexity: O(n), Space complexity: O(1)
If fast reaches NULL, no cycle exists

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a singly linked list with the following operations: (a) Create a node (b) Insert at beginning (c) Insert at end (d) Delete from beginning (e) Display the list.
keyPoints: Define structure for node with data and next pointer
Implement createNode function with malloc
Insert at beginning: update head pointer
Insert at end: traverse to last node then add
Delete from beginning: update head to next
Display: traverse from head until NULL
Handle edge cases: empty list, single node

questions.question: Explain the process of reversing a singly linked list. Write an algorithm for the same and state its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Three pointers needed: previous, current, next
Initialize: prev=NULL, current=head
While current is not NULL: save next, reverse pointer, move prev and current
At end, set head to prev
Time complexity: O(n) - single traversal
Space complexity: O(1) - only three pointers used
In-place reversal without extra memory

questions.question: Write a Python program to implement a doubly linked list with insertion operations at the beginning, end, and after a given node.
keyPoints: Define Node class with prev, data, next attributes
DoublyLinkedList class with head initialization
Insert at beginning: connect new node to current head
Insert at end: traverse to last node, connect accordingly
Insert after node: update all four pointers (new's prev/next, node's next, next's prev)
Handle empty list case separately
Return appropriate references

questions.question: Compare arrays and linked lists as data structures. Discuss scenarios where one is preferred over the other.
keyPoints: Arrays: contiguous memory, fixed size, O(1) random access, O(n) insertion/deletion
Linked lists: non-contiguous, dynamic size, O(n) access, O(1) insertion/deletion at known position
Arrays preferred: when random access is frequent, size is known, memory is limited
Linked lists preferred: when size is unknown, frequent insertions/deletions, memory efficiency needed
Arrays: cache-friendly, linked lists: not cache-friendly
Arrays: no pointer overhead, linked lists: pointer overhead
